g.data-narration: Welcome to this lesson on Using Super in Java. We'll explore how to leverage the super keyword for inheritance.
The super keyword refers to the superclass, allowing access to constructors and members. It's essential for proper inheritance.
g.text: The super keyword refers to the
Used to access constructors and members.

g.data-narration: In inheritance, the superclass provides properties, and the subclass extends them. This relationship is foundational.
Use super() to call the superclass constructor. It must be the first statement in the subclass constructor for proper initialization.
g.text: • Must be the FIRST statement in subclass constructor
⚠ Must call super() explicitly when superclass has parameterized constructor

g.data-narration: When creating an object, the superclass constructor runs first, then the subclass constructor completes initialization.
Use super.memberName to access overridden methods or hidden fields in the superclass when needed.
When a subclass overrides a method, super.methodName lets you call the original version from the parent class.
Here's a complete example showing super() calling the parent constructor and initializing subclass fields.
This example demonstrates super.methodName() to call the parent version while adding subclass-specific behavior.
Using super ensures proper initialization and prevents code duplication. Without it, the no-arg constructor is implicitly called.
g.text: Benefits: Using Super vs Not Using Super

g.data-narration: Remember these two key syntax forms: super() for constructors and super.member for accessing parent members.
For exams, identify super in code snippets and explain its purpose. Practice designing class hierarchies using super.
In summary, super is essential for proper inheritance: call constructors with super() and access parent members with super.memberName.
Thank you for learning about Using Super in Java. Practice these concepts to master inheritance in object-oriented programming.
